title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune (Fragment 3)
text:
There was a lot of time for thinking, because Bessie duly left to be married and Sophie was mostly alone in the shop. Fanny did seem to be out a lot, gadding or not, and trade was slack after May Day. After three days Sophie plucked up courage to ask Fanny, “Shouldn’t I be earning a wage?”

“Of course, my love, with all you do!” Fanny answered warmly, fixing on a rose-trimmed hat in front of the shop mirror. “We’ll see about it as soon as I’ve done the accounts this evening.” Then she went out and did not come back until Sophie had shut the shop and taken that day’s hats through to the house to trim.

Sophie at first felt mean to have listened to Martha, but when Fanny did not mention a wage, either that evening or any time later that week, Sophie began to think that Martha had been right.

“Maybe I am being exploited,” she told a hat she was trimming with red silk and a bunch of wax cherries, “but someone has to do this or there will be no hats at all to sell.” She finished that hat and started on a stark black-and-white one, very modish, and a quite new thought came to her. “Does it matter if there are no hats to sell?” she asked it. She looked round the assembled hats, on stands or waiting in a heap to be trimmed. “What good are you all?” she asked them. “You certainly aren’t doing me a scrap of good.”

And she was within an ace of leaving the house and setting out to seek her fortune, until she remembered she was the eldest and there was no point. She took up the hat again, sighing.

She was still discontented, alone in the shop next morning, when a very plain young woman customer stormed in, whirling a pleated mushroom bonnet by its ribbons. “Look at this!” the young lady shrieked. “You told me this was the same as the bonnet Jane Farrier was wearing when she met the Count. And you lied. Nothing has happened to me at all!”

“I’m not surprised,” Sophie said, before she had caught up with herself. “If you’re fool enough to wear that bonnet with a face like that, you wouldn’t have the wit to spot the King himself if he came begging—if he hadn’t turned to stone first just at the sight of you.”

The customer glared. Then she threw the bonnet at Sophie and stormed out of the shop. Sophie carefully crammed the bonnet into the wastebasket, panting rather. The rule was: Lose your temper, lose a customer. She had just proven that rule. It troubled her to realize how very enjoyable it had been.

Sophie had no time to recover. There was the sound of wheels and horse hoofs and a carriage darkened the window. The shop bell clanged and the grandest customer she had ever seen sailed in, with a sable wrap drooping from her elbows and diamonds winking all over her dense black dress. Sophie’s eyes went to the lady’s wide hat first—real ostrich plume dyed to reflect the pinks and greens and blues winking in the diamonds and yet still look black. This was a wealthy hat. The lady’s face was carefully beautiful. The chestnut-brown hair made her seem young, but … Sophie’s eyes took in the young man who followed the lady in, a slightly formless-faced person with reddish hair, quite well dressed, but pale and obviously upset. He stared at Sophie with a kind of beseeching horror. He was clearly younger than the lady. Sophie was puzzled.

“Miss Hatter?” the lady asked in a musical but commanding voice.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune

vocabulary.definition: Financial records of income and expenses.
vocabulary.example: He checked the accounts to balance the budget.

vocabulary.definition: Brought together in one place for a common purpose.
vocabulary.example: The assembled crowd waited eagerly for the speech.

vocabulary.definition: Imploring or begging earnestly.
vocabulary.example: She gave him a beseeching look to reconsider.

vocabulary.definition: Having a powerful or authoritative presence or tone.
vocabulary.example: He spoke in a commanding tone that demanded attention.

vocabulary.definition: Not satisfied; unhappy with a situation.
vocabulary.example: The workers were discontented with their pay.

vocabulary.definition: Hanging or bending downwards.
vocabulary.example: The flowers were drooping from lack of water.

vocabulary.definition: To receive payment for work done, typically regular pay.
vocabulary.example: She works hard to earn her wage.

vocabulary.definition: Treated unfairly or used selfishly for one's own advantage.
vocabulary.example: The workers felt exploited by the factory owner.

vocabulary.definition: Move around or go from place to place aimlessly or frequently.
vocabulary.example: Fanny was often out, gadding about in town.

vocabulary.definition: To look at someone fiercely or with anger.
vocabulary.example: She glared angrily at the rude comment.

vocabulary.definition: To become angry or lose control of one's emotions.
vocabulary.example: If you lose your temper, you'll regret it later.

vocabulary.definition: Unkind or spiteful in behaviour or attitude.
vocabulary.example: It was mean of him to tease her like that.

vocabulary.definition: Conforming to or following what is currently popular or fashionable.
vocabulary.example: She wore a modish outfit to the party.

vocabulary.definition: A type of bonnet shaped round and full, resembling a mushroom cap.
vocabulary.example: The vintage shop had a display of mushroom bonnets.

vocabulary.definition: A decorative feather from an ostrich, often used in hat making.
vocabulary.example: The hat was adorned with a large ostrich plume.

vocabulary.definition: Breathing quickly and heavily, often from exertion or stress.
vocabulary.example: The dog was panting after the long run.

vocabulary.definition: Having folds sewn or pressed into fabric repeatedly.
vocabulary.example: She wore a pleated skirt to the event.

vocabulary.definition: To find the confidence to do something difficult or frightening.
vocabulary.example: He finally plucked up courage to speak in public.

vocabulary.definition: Demonstrated to be true or valid by evidence or argument.
vocabulary.example: The experiment proved the hypothesis correct.

vocabulary.definition: Black in colour; also refers to an expensive fur from the sable marten.
vocabulary.example: She wore a luxurious sable coat.

vocabulary.definition: To utter a loud, high-pitched scream or cry.
vocabulary.example: She shrieked in surprise when she saw the spider.

vocabulary.definition: Not busy or active; reduced activity or demand.
vocabulary.example: Business became slack during the holiday season.

vocabulary.definition: A container for discarding waste paper or rubbish.
vocabulary.example: He threw the crumpled paper into the wastebasket.

vocabulary.definition: Artificial or decorative cherries made of wax, often used for ornamentation.
vocabulary.example: The hat was decorated with wax cherries.

vocabulary.definition: The ability to perceive and understand quickly; intelligence or cleverness.
vocabulary.example: He showed great wit in solving the puzzle.

vocabulary.definition: Very close to doing something; almost.
vocabulary.example: He was within an ace of winning the race.
